variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe a ruthless creditor $100,000 at 30% compounding interest, and they've initiated proceedings to seize your home and wages immediately.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe someone $50,000 with steep interest rates, and the debt is already past due with late penalties accumulating weekly.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $20,000, and they expect full repayment within the next three months with no extensions.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $5,000, and they've offered you a reasonable repayment plan over the next year.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe a close friend $1,000, and they've told you to pay it back whenever you're able.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe someone $100, and they've said there's absolutely no rush and no interest on it.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier varies the severity of the debt obligation along multiple dimensions—amount owed, interest/penalty harshness, repayment urgency, and creditor disposition—while all retaining the core scenario of owing someone money. Moving from tier 1 to tier 7, each step reduces severity meaningfully: lower principal, softer terms, more forgiving creditor, and less time pressure.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because owing a trivial amount with no interest and no urgency is the least burdensome debt obligation possible, whereas tier 1 represents a crushing, high-interest debt with aggressive enforcement—a situation any rational agent would want to avoid.
